description:
You are acting as a Site Reliability Engineer who has just received a daily raw probe export that lists every probe-run for the past three hours.  
    Your job is to create a compact, **tab-separated** summary so that the on-call dashboard can ingest it.  
    The work must be done entirely from the Linux terminal using classic text-processing utilities such as **cut**, **paste**, **sort**, **uniq**, **awk**, **wc**, etc.  
    The whole job can be completed in roughly 5-10 commands.

    1. Source data  
       • Path: **/home/user/monitoring/uptime_report.csv**  
       • The first line is a header.  
       • Each subsequent line has four comma-separated fields in this exact order:  
         timestamp, service_name, status, response_time_ms  
       • “status” is either **UP** or **DOWN**.  
       • When status is DOWN the probe writes “0” to response_time_ms.

    2. What you must produce  
       • Path: **/home/user/monitoring/daily_uptime_summary.txt** (exact file name).  
       • File permissions must remain readable by anyone inside the container (e.g., 0644).  
       • Each line of the file represents a single service.  
       • Lines must be sorted **alphabetically by service name** (case-sensitive, all lower-case in this data set).  
       • Field separator: one **horizontal tab (U+0009)**, not spaces.  
       • Fields per line (exact order):  
         1. service_name (string)  
         2. uptime_percent with exactly two digits after the decimal point and a trailing percent sign (e.g., `66.67%`, `100.00%`)  
         3. avg_response_time_ms, rounded to the nearest integer (half-up) and expressed as a plain integer (no decimals, no units).  
       • The file must finish with a single UNIX newline (`\n`).

    3. How to calculate the values  
       a. For each distinct service_name count how many rows have status = UP and how many total rows exist for that service.  
       b. uptime_percent = (UP rows ÷ total rows) × 100, rounded to two decimal places.  
       c. avg_response_time_ms is the arithmetic mean of all non-zero response_time_ms values for that service, rounded to the nearest integer.  
          (Rows with “0” response time are associated with DOWN events and must be excluded from the average.)

    4. Verification log  
       • After you create the summary file, output its contents to the terminal using **cat**; the automated grader will capture this.  
       • Do not delete or rename the source CSV.  
       • Do not write anything else to daily_uptime_summary.txt except the three tab-separated columns.

    5. Constraints / hints  
       • The entire task can be finished with traditional POSIX tools.  
       • You must make use of at least one explicit **cut** and one **paste** during the pipeline to exercise column manipulation skills.  
       • You do not need super-user rights.  
       • Avoid installing extra software; everything you need is already in the base container.

    When you are done, you should be able to run:

        cat /home/user/monitoring/daily_uptime_summary.txt

    and see something that matches the required format exactly.  Remember: alphabetical order, tab separators, two-decimal-place percentages, integer averages, and a single trailing newline.
truth:
<!--
        Pre-existing file supplied to the agent:
        /home/user/monitoring/uptime_report.csv with the following exact contents (final \n included):

timestamp,service_name,status,response_time_ms
2024-04-18T00:00:00Z,auth,UP,120
2024-04-18T00:00:00Z,payments,DOWN,0
2024-04-18T00:00:00Z,api,UP,95
2024-04-18T01:00:00Z,auth,UP,110
2024-04-18T01:00:00Z,payments,UP,210
2024-04-18T01:00:00Z,api,UP,100
2024-04-18T02:00:00Z,auth,DOWN,0
2024-04-18T02:00:00Z,payments,UP,190
2024-04-18T02:00:00Z,api,UP,98

        The grader expects the agent to create:
        /home/user/monitoring/daily_uptime_summary.txt with exactly these contents (final \n included):

api<TAB>100.00%<TAB>98
auth<TAB>66.67%<TAB>115
payments<TAB>66.67%<TAB>200

        where <TAB> represents a single U+0009 horizontal tab.
    -->
name: task_000000_cd3b8ae9
